does: This paper introduces conditional training, a pretraining objective that learns to generate tokens conditioned on their human preference scores from a reward model. The authors benchmark five alternative pretraining objectives alongside standard next-token prediction across three tasks, measuring trade-offs between alignment and capability. Their main finding is that conditional training achieves Pareto-optimal performance: it reduces undesirable content generation by up to ten-fold in both unprompted and adversarially-prompted settings while maintaining downstream task performance before and after finetuning. The approach effectively steers language models away from falsehoods, offensive content, personally identifiable information, low-quality code, and other human-dispreferred outputs during the pretraining phase itself.
connects: This work builds on the line of research combining language model pretraining with human preferences and alignment, extending beyond post-hoc alignment methods like reinforcement learning from human feedback that only apply after pretraining. It responds to the observation that standard internet-text pretraining inherently captures undesirable content and behaviors, connecting to prior work on reward modeling and preference learning. The paper engages with fundamental questions about training objectives raised by constitutional AI and alignment research, while drawing on recent work demonstrating that human feedback signals can guide model behavior. The conditional training approach synthesizes ideas from controlled text generation and preference-conditioned language modeling, addressing the gap between pretraining-time and fine-tuning-time alignment.
enables: This work opens pathways for more efficient and principled approaches to LM alignment by pushing preference-based guidance earlier into the training pipeline. It suggests that human preferences can be incorporated at pretraining scale without sacrificing capabilities, potentially reducing reliance on expensive post-hoc alignment procedures. The findings enable future research into multi-objective pretraining objectives that simultaneously optimize for helpfulness, harmlessness, and honesty. The approach points toward developing better reward models that can operate at pretraining scale and exploring how different preference signals and training dynamics interact. Results also motivate investigating whether conditional training generalizes across diverse domains and preference distributions, and whether similar principles apply to other modalities or model architectures.
